questions.question: Why is using a CDN not the end of the design discussion?
options: Because you still need to decide cache keys, invalidation, freshness, and origin-protection behavior.
Because CDNs only work for images.
Because cache layers remove all consistency concerns.
Because browsers cannot cache responses.
questions.explanation: The hard parts are often policy and correctness, not the label 'CDN.'

questions.question: What is a common reason to add a shield or tiered cache layer?
options: To collapse many edge misses before they reach origin and reduce fan-in.
To make purges impossible.
To ensure every request visits more data centers than necessary.
To bypass all cache-control headers.
questions.explanation: Shield layers reduce duplicate origin misses from many POPs.

questions.question: Which workload benefits most cleanly from long-lived versioned URLs?
options: Immutable static assets such as JS bundles, CSS, and images.
Per-user personalized dashboards.
Write-heavy control-plane APIs.
Admin mutation endpoints.
questions.explanation: Versioned URLs make invalidation simple when content is immutable after publish.

questions.question: What is the main risk of a poorly designed cache key?
options: Cache fragmentation or accidental sharing of the wrong content variant.
Automatic elimination of all purge traffic.
Guaranteed improvement in origin protection.
The inability to use TLS at the edge.
questions.explanation: Bad keys either miss too often or serve unsafe content across users or variants.

questions.question: Why can purge storms become a system-design problem?
options: They can overload the invalidation control plane and delay freshness updates globally.
They increase browser battery usage only.
They remove the need for tags or prefixes.
They only matter when the origin is a database.
questions.explanation: Frequent purges create their own scale and propagation challenges.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals whether tiered caching is protecting origin effectively?
options: Origin request collapse rate and origin QPS reduction on cacheable traffic.
The number of tabs open in the browser.
Only global average CPU on developer laptops.
The count of markdown files in the repo.
questions.explanation: Tiered caching is valuable when it measurably reduces duplicate origin fetches.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with selective caching of dynamic fragments?
options: Lower latency and origin load at the cost of more cache-key and invalidation complexity.
Perfect correctness with zero policy work.
Removal of all observability needs.
Guaranteed support for every personalization case.
questions.explanation: Dynamic caching can help a lot, but only if policy remains precise.

questions.question: What is the best first response when shared caches accidentally serve personalized content?
options: Separate personalized routes and tighten cache-key and cacheability rules immediately.
Increase TTL so users see the leak for less time.
Disable logs to reduce noise.
Move all content to one region.
questions.explanation: This is a correctness and privacy issue driven by policy boundaries.
